questions.question: Explain CPU Utilization and Throughput as scheduling criteria. How are they related?
keyPoints: CPU Utilization measures percentage of time CPU is busy executing processes, typically 40-90%
Throughput is number of processes completed per unit time
They are positively related: higher CPU utilization generally leads to higher throughput
But optimal utilization doesn't always mean maximum throughput due to overhead

questions.question: Differentiate between Waiting Time and Response Time with examples.
keyPoints: Waiting Time: total time process spends in ready queue before execution
Response Time: time from arrival to first CPU execution
Example: A process arrives at t=0, gets CPU at t=5: response time=5, if it runs from t=5-8, waiting time includes any additional queue time
Response time critical for interactive systems, waiting time affects turnaround

questions.question: What is Turnaround Time? How is it calculated?
keyPoints: Turnaround time = Completion Time - Arrival Time
Includes: Waiting time + CPU execution time + I/O time
Example: Process arrives at t=0, completes at t=20, turnaround = 20 units
Minimizing turnaround time is crucial for batch processing systems

questions.question: Discuss the importance of Fairness in CPU scheduling and explain how starvation relates to fairness.
keyPoints: Fairness ensures every process gets reasonable CPU time share
Without fairness, some processes may starve (never get CPU)
Starvation: process waits indefinitely due to continuous arrival of higher-priority processes
Aging prevents starvation by gradually increasing priority of waiting processes

questions.question: Compare the scheduling criteria priorities for: (a) Batch systems (b) Interactive systems (c) Real-time systems
keyPoints: Batch: Maximize throughput, minimize turnaround time - focus on efficiency
Interactive: Minimize response time - user perceives system speed
Real-time: Meet deadlines - predictability more important than speed
Trade-offs exist: optimizing one criterion may worsen others

questions.question: Define CPU burst and explain the difference between CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes with respect to scheduling.
keyPoints: CPU burst: continuous time process executes on CPU before I/O or interruption
CPU-bound process: long CPU bursts, less I/O, benefits from longer time slices
I/O-bound process: short CPU bursts, frequent I/O, responds better to shorter time quantum
Scheduler must balance between both types for optimal CPU utilization

questions.question: Consider three processes P1, P2, P3 with burst times 10, 5, and 8 respectively, arriving at time 0. Calculate average waiting time and average turnaround time for: (a) FCFS (b) SJF (c) Round Robin with time quantum = 4
keyPoints: FCFS: P1 waits 0, P2 waits 10, P3 waits 15. Avg waiting = 25/3 = 8.33. Turnaround: P1=10, P2=15, P3=23. Avg = 48/3 = 16
SJF (non-preemptive): Order P2(5), P3(8), P1(10). Waiting: P2=0, P3=5, P1=13. Avg = 18/3 = 6. Turnaround: P2=5, P3=13, P1=23. Avg = 41/3 = 13.67
Round Robin: Time quantum 4. Complete schedule: P1(4), P2(4), P3(4), P1(4), P2(1), P3(4), P1(2). Calculate waiting and turnaround from completion times
Shows SJF optimizes waiting/turnaround but can cause starvation, RR provides fairness

questions.question: Explain the concept of scheduling conflicts. How do different scheduling criteria sometimes conflict with each other? Give examples.
keyPoints: Scheduling conflict: optimizing one criterion degrades another
Response time vs Throughput: Smaller time quantum in RR improves response but increases context switches, reducing throughput
Waiting time vs Fairness: SJF minimizes waiting but may starve long jobs
Turnaround time vs CPU Utilization: Preemptive scheduling may reduce efficiency due to overhead
No single algorithm optimizes all criteria - design depends on system type
Trade-off decisions reflect system priorities (batch vs interactive vs real-time)

questions.question: Describe the five standard CPU scheduling criteria. For each criterion, identify one system type (batch, interactive, real-time) where it is most critical and explain why.
keyPoints: 1. CPU Utilization: Keep CPU busy - important for all systems, especially batch for efficiency
2. Throughput: Processes completed per unit time - critical for batch systems processing large workloads
3. Turnaround Time: Total time from arrival to completion - batch systems want jobs done quickly
4. Waiting Time: Time spent in ready queue - affects user perception in interactive systems
5. Response Time: Time to first execution - most critical for interactive and real-time systems
Additional: Fairness prevents starvation - important for multi-user systems
Each system type prioritizes criteria based on user needs and operational requirements

questions.question: Define and explain: (i) Starvation (ii) Convoy Effect (iii) Aging. How does each relate to scheduling criteria?
keyPoints: Starvation: Process waits indefinitely because other processes continuously get priority - violates fairness criterion
Convoy Effect: CPU-bound processes cause I/O-bound processes to wait, reducing CPU utilization and throughput
Aging: Technique where process priority increases with waiting time - prevents starvation, improves fairness
Starvation directly conflicts with fairness criterion, violates fairness
Convoy effect reduces CPU utilization and throughput by creating artificial bottlenecks
Aging addresses fairness by dynamically adjusting priorities, indirectly improving waiting time for starved processes
All concepts demonstrate complexity of meeting multiple scheduling criteria simultaneously
